narrative: As a Black male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 26.50—both solid but below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students—the admissions model took a close look at your full profile, including your upper-middle family income background and your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st out of 10. It also noted your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students in the dataset. The model predicts a 41.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, a risk high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize students with stronger projected odds of passing. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that most strongly shaped the prediction. Your undergraduate GPA stood out as the top positive influence, pushing the outlook toward bar passage because it's right in line with historical averages for successful students. Similarly, your upper-middle family income was a strong favorable factor, as it aligns with backgrounds that have correlated with better bar outcomes in the past. However, your low Year 1 decile ranking—1st out of 10, well below the average 6th decile—emerged as the main driver tipping the scales toward a failure prediction, signaling early academic struggles that outweighed those positives for this model. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early grades pulled you into a riskier group, even with your average GPA and common full-time status. While your LSAT was a modest drag, it wasn't among the top influencers here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar starts have succeeded—but the model focuses on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the average, strengthening your early college performance through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications, and targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background. With targeted improvements, you can shift future predictions in your favor and get back on track for law school.
